notes: Integrated from the real Grade 2 Quarter 2, Book 11 source file.
Developing card: moderate length with more vocabulary and plot detail.

book.title: The Little Match Girl
book.description: The Little Match Girl includes a six-chapter reading sequence, vocabulary, comprehension questions, journal/writing prompts, and assessment checks.

choiceGuidance.reason: moderate length with more vocabulary and plot detail

weeks.title: The Little Match Girl Book Card
reading: Chapter 1: A Cold New Year's Eve
vocabulary: 1. desperately - with great need and urgency; in a hopeless way
2. shivering - shaking from cold or fear
1. harsh - very severe and difficult to bear
1. unbearable - too difficult or painful to accept or endure
2. radiating - spreading out in all directions from a central point
1. dwindling - becoming smaller or fewer; running out
1. majestic - very grand and impressive; having great dignity
1. miraculous - amazing and wonderful; like a miracle
2. compassion - deep care and sympathy for someone who is suffering
journal: Write a kind response to the girl from the point of view of someone who sees her.
Describe the mood of the story and name details that create that feeling.
writing: Write a kind response to the girl from the point of view of someone who sees her.
Describe the mood of the story and name details that create that feeling.
questions: 1. What was the little girl trying to sell?
2. Why was the little girl afraid to go home?
1. Where did the little girl find shelter?
2. What could the little girl see from her hiding place?
1. Why did the little girl decide to light a match?
2. What vision did she see in the match flame?
1. What happened when the first match burned out?
2. What did the little girl see in the second match?
1. What did the little girl see in the third match?
2. Who did the Christmas tree remind the little girl of?
1. Who appeared to the little girl in the fourth match?
2. How was the little match girl found the next morning?
assessment: Use chapter comprehension questions for daily checks.
Use journal/writing prompt as written-response evidence.
